metadata.title: Consumer Fraud Protection
metadata.subtitle: Recognize, Prevent, and Respond to Scams

content.title: Consumer Fraud Protection
content.subtitle: Recognize, Prevent, and Respond to Scams

content.label: The Challenge
content.question:
Alex received an urgent email from "BankofAmerica1@secure-verify.net" claiming suspicious activity on his account.

The email looked professional—complete with the bank's logo and colors—and demanded he click a link to verify his SSN and password within 24 hours or his account would be frozen.

How can Alex tell if this email is legitimate, and what should he do?

objectives.description: Identify common types of consumer fraud including phishing, spoofing, identity theft, and Ponzi schemes.
Recognize warning signs and red flags that indicate potential fraud attempts.
Apply prevention strategies to protect personal and financial information from scammers.
Respond appropriately if you become a victim of fraud, knowing where to report and how to recover.

terms.definition: Fraudulent emails or messages designed to trick you into revealing sensitive information like passwords, SSN, or bank details.
terms.example: An email pretending to be from your bank asking you to 'verify your account' through a fake link.

terms.definition: Impersonating legitimate companies or people through fake emails, websites, or caller IDs to gain your trust.
terms.example: A caller ID showing 'IRS' when it's actually a scammer trying to steal your information.

terms.definition: Using stolen personal information to open accounts, make purchases, or commit other fraud in someone else's name.
terms.example: A thief uses your SSN and birthdate to open credit cards, leaving you with the debt and damaged credit.

terms.definition: Manipulating people into revealing confidential information through psychological tactics like urgency, fear, or trust-building.
terms.example: A scammer builds an online friendship for weeks before asking you to invest in a 'guaranteed' opportunity.

leftPanel.title: Scammers Are Getting Smarter
paragraphs: Today's scammers create professional-looking emails, websites, and calls that seem completely legitimate.
They exploit urgency ('Act now!'), fear ('Your account will be closed!'), and greed ('You've won!').
The best defense is verification: contact companies directly through official channels, never through links in unsolicited messages.

highlightBox.text: Legitimate organizations NEVER ask for passwords or SSN via email or text.

layoutData.title: Know What You're Protecting Against
paragraphs: Fraud comes in many forms, but they all rely on the same goal: getting you to reveal information or money.
bulletPoints: Phishing: Fake emails/texts that trick you into giving up passwords or financial info
Spoofing: Disguising communications to appear from trusted companies or government
Catfishing: Fake online identities used to build trust before requesting money
Ponzi Schemes: 'Investment' scams paying old investors with new investor money
Credit Repair Scams: Companies charging fees for 'credit fixes' they can't deliver
Tech Support Scams: Fake warnings claiming your computer is infected

keyPoint.text: 💡 All scams share one thing: they want you to act FAST before you can think clearly or verify.

leftColumn.title: Warning Signs
items: Requests for SSN, passwords, or PINs
Pressure to wire money or use gift cards

rightColumn.title: Legitimate Communications
items: Never ask for passwords via email

layoutData.title: Build Your Defense System
paragraphs: Protecting yourself requires both good habits and quick action when something seems wrong.
bulletPoints: Verify independently: Contact companies directly via official channels—never click links in messages
Use strong passwords: Unique passwords for each account, with two-factor authentication enabled
Monitor your accounts: Check bank statements regularly, set up transaction alerts
Limit social sharing: Don't post birthdates, addresses, or vacation plans publicly
Check credit reports: Free annual reports from each bureau at AnnualCreditReport.com
Use credit freezes: Prevents anyone from opening accounts in your name

keyPoint.text: 🔐 A credit freeze is FREE and stops 90% of identity theft—unfreeze when YOU need credit.

leftPanel.title: Act Fast—Every Hour Matters
paragraphs: Step 1: Contact your bank immediately to freeze accounts and dispute charges.
Step 2: Change all compromised passwords—use a password manager.
Step 3: Document everything: save emails, take screenshots, note dates.
Step 4: Report to the FTC at ReportFraud.ftc.gov.
Step 5: Place fraud alerts with all three credit bureaus.
Step 6: File a police report for identity theft cases.

leftPanel.title: You're Not Immune
paragraphs: Contrary to popular belief, young people aged 18-29 report higher fraud losses than any other age group.
Why? Digital natives are more trusting of online communications and may not verify as carefully.
Social media provides scammers with personal details they use to craft convincing attacks.

highlightBox.text: Young adults lose more money per incident because they're more likely to use payment apps and cryptocurrency.

stats.label: Increase in fraud targeting young adults
Average time to recover from ID theft

content.headerTitle: The IRS and Banks: What They Will Never Do

layoutData.title: Know the Rules Legitimate Organizations Follow
paragraphs: Understanding how real institutions communicate helps you spot fakes instantly.
bulletPoints: The IRS will NEVER: Text you, email you about refunds, demand immediate payment via gift cards, or threaten arrest
Your bank will NEVER: Ask for your password via email, demand you verify your SSN through a link, or threaten account closure via text
Legitimate investments will NEVER: Guarantee returns, pressure you to act immediately, or ask for wire transfers
Government agencies will NEVER: Accept payment in cryptocurrency or gift cards

keyPoint.text: 📞 When in doubt, hang up and call the official number on your card or statement—never the number they give you.

scenario.name: Alex's Phishing Email
paragraphs: Alex received an email from 'BankofAmerica1@secure-verify.net' with an urgent subject line: 'URGENT: Verify Your Account.'
The email included the bank's logo and asked him to click a link to confirm his SSN and password within 24 hours or his account would be frozen.

outcomes.label: Red Flag #3
outcomes.detail: Banks never ask for this via email

scenario.name: Miguel's Investment 'Opportunity'
paragraphs: Miguel met someone online claiming to be a financial advisor. After weeks of friendly chatting, they offered him a 'guaranteed 50% return' on an investment.
The 'advisor' asked Miguel to wire $5,000 to get started, promising his money would double within 6 months.

outcomes.label: Red Flag #3
outcomes.detail: Trust built over weeks before the ask

content.question:
Jessica received this text: 'IRS Notice: You are eligible for a $1,247 tax refund. Claim now at www.irs-refund-claim.com. Enter your SSN and bank routing number.'

What are ALL the red flags in this message?

Why might someone fall for this even though the signs are obvious when pointed out?

Think about an older relative or friend who might be vulnerable to scams.

How would you explain phishing to them in a supportive, non-condescending way?

What ONE specific habit would you encourage them to develop?

questions.question: What are THREE red flags that indicate an email might be a phishing attempt?
Explain why the IRS would NEVER contact you by text message asking for your Social Security number.
What should Alex (from The Challenge) do instead of clicking the link in the email?
Why are 'guaranteed returns' on investments ALWAYS a warning sign of potential fraud?

takeaways.title: Know the Fraud Types
takeaways.description: Phishing, spoofing, identity theft, and Ponzi schemes all aim to steal your money or information—recognize each.

takeaways.title: Spot the Red Flags
takeaways.description: Unofficial domains, urgency tactics, SSN requests, and 'guaranteed' returns are universal warning signs.

takeaways.title: Verify Before You Act
takeaways.description: Contact companies directly through official channels—never click links in unsolicited messages.

takeaways.title: Know How to Respond
takeaways.description: If victimized, freeze accounts immediately, report to FTC, place fraud alerts, and document everything.

main.title: Fraud Investigation Lab
main.description: You've learned to spot red flags—but can you investigate like a fraud analyst? Tomorrow you'll examine real-world scam examples, trace the manipulation tactics, and build your personal protection system.

steps: Analyze authentic phishing emails and identify ALL warning signs
Research the latest scam techniques targeting your age group
Create a personal fraud prevention checklist
Draft a 'scam alert' script to help protect family members
Build your verification workflow for suspicious communications

content.tagline: Building Financial Futures, One Lesson at a Time
